MINI MINUTES:

Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-30-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES VIA COURTCALL: Atty. BLAKE P. BOSTWICK, Esq. of Bostwick & Peterson, LLP (Tel: 415-421-8300) for plaintiff;
Atty. JULIA HANSEN-ARENAS, Esq. of Hassard Bonnington LLP (Tel: 415-288-9800) for defendants.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS JOCELYN CHAPMAN & THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendants Dr. Chapman and The Regents of the University of California's motion to strike is granted in part and denied in part. Plaintiff does not oppose defendants' motion to strike the terms "reckless" and "conscious disregard to her safety" from paragraph 12 of the first amended complaint. The motion is denied as to the terms "careless" and "carelessly". These terms are neither irrelevant nor immaterial to a negligence cause of action. (Code Civ. Proc., 410.10(b); see Pleuso v. City Taxi Co. (1919) 41 Cal.App. 297.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-30-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES VIA COURTCALL: Atty. BLAKE P. BOSTWICK, Esq. of Bostwick & Peterson, LLP (Tel: 415-421-8300) for plaintiff;
Atty. JULIA HANSEN-ARENAS, Esq. of Hassard Bonnington LLP (Tel: 415-288-9800) for defendants.

HEARING RE DEFTS DR. CHAPMAN & THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendants Dr. Chapman & The Regents of the University of California's demurrer to the second and third causes of action of plaintiff's first amended complaint is overruled. Medical battery occurs when a physician performs a medical procedure or treatment without the patient's consent or performs a procedure that is substantially different from the one consented to. (Cobbs v. Grant (1972) 8 Cal.3d 229, 239 [medical battery occurs where "a doctor obtains consent of the patient to perform one type of treatment and subsequently performs a substantially different treatment for which consent was not obtained"]; Saxena v. Goffney (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 316, 324.) Although plaintiff pleads that she conditionally consented to the procedure, she now disclaims pursuing a claim for medical battery under the conditional consent theory, but instead seeks to state a claim for battery under CACI 530A [defendant performed a medical procedure without plaintiff's consent; or that plaintiff consented to one medical procedure, but defendant performed a substantially different medical procedure]. Plaintiff's two arguments under this theory are that she withdrew her consent when she complained about the pain and that the procedure she consented to was "substantially different" than the one Dr. Chapman performed. Plaintiff alleges she consented to a vaginal colposcopy procedure on August 17, 2018. (FAC 6.) Plaintiff's withdrawal of consent argument fails because whether plaintiff communicated to defendant that she withdrew her consent, and Dr. Chapman had a duty to stop the procedure, sounds in negligence, not battery. (See Saxena v. Goffney, 159 Cal.App.4th at 324.) However, whether the procedure Dr. Chapman actually performed was substantially different than the vaginal colposcopy to which plaintiff consented presents a question of fact; thus, plaintiff's medical battery cause of action is sufficiently pled. (See Kaplan v. Mamelak (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 637, 646 ["[T]here is no overarching test for deciding whether a medical procedure is 'substantially different' from the authorized procedure."].) Plaintiff alleges there are three types of colposcopy procedures and that she only consented to one type, vaginal. (FAC 9, 18, 24, 26; see Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Co. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 604 [no matter how unlikely or improbable, plaintiff's allegations must be accepted as true for the purpose of ruling on the demurrer].) Plaintiff adequately pleads The Regents' vicarious liability. The court denies defendants' request on reply that it take judicial notice of certain medical terminology, which is untimely and is not dispositive of the parties' factual disagreement in any event. Plaintiff also sufficiently pleads a cause of action for willful misconduct. (Carlsen v. Koivumaki (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 879, 895 [willful misconduct is an aggravated form of negligence].) Plaintiff alleges that as of 2013, The Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) recommended that undiluted (glacial) acetic acid neither be stored nor used in clinical medicine. (FAC 10.) ISMP warned that using glacial acetic acid could cause serious patient harm including pain, serious tissue damage, and third-degree burns. (Id.) Plaintiff alleges that despite this warning to defendants, they continued to stock full strength glacial acetic acid in clinical areas and utilized it in plaintiff's procedure. (FAC 11.) Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com (302/EPS)
